coredata.dc:title: Manipulating microenvironments of nanochannels in lamellar membranes by quantum dots for highly enhanced nanofiltration performance
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Xiaoli
Liu, Shiyuan
Cui, Xulin
Lin, Jianlong
Zhang, Haoqin
Zhang, Jie
Wang, Jingtao
coredata.dc:description: Lamellar membranes are promising for highly selective permeation, among which graphene-based ones are widely studied. However, graphene-based membranes give limited molecule permeance due to the unfavorable microenvironments of nanochannels, mainly single chemical affinity and narrow channel size. Herein, quantum dots (QDs) were introduced into nanochannels to manipulate the microenvironments for optimized performance. Particularly, hydrophobic graphene quantum dots were anchored in hydrophilic graphene oxide nanochannels to improve the affinity towards nonpolar molecules and locally enlarge the channel size from 0.84 to 1.35 nm. The pleasant affinity facilitates rapid dissolution and entry of molecules into nanochannels, and the enlarged channel size promotes their fast transport. This synergistic effect permits remarkably enhanced permeance for both polar and nonpolar molecules, especially for nonpolar ones. Meanwhile, the narrow distance at QD-free area effectively rejects dyes larger than 2.0 nm. Additionally, the strong covalent bonds between QDs and adjacent nanosheets bring superior stability for membranes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene-based lamellar membrane
Quantum dot
Nanochannel microenvironment
Molecule transfer
Organic solvent nanofiltration

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of GO, RGO, GQDs, and PQDs
Preparation of GQD@GO and PQD@RGO lamellar composite membranes
Synthesis and characterization of GQD@GO and PQD@RGO composites
Morphologies and physicochemical properties of lamellar composite membranes
Nanofiltration performance of lamellar composite membranes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the preparation of GQD@GO composite and membrane.

Proofs for the synthesis of GQD@GO and PQD@RGO composites. TEM images of (a) GO nanosheet, (b) GQD, and (c) GQD@GO composite. (d) C 1s spectra of GO (upper) and PQD@GO composite (below). (e) C 1s spectra of PQDs (upper) and GQDs (below). (f) FTIR spectra of PQDs, GQDs, GO, RGO, GQD@GO, and PQD@RGO composites.

Proofs for the preparation of membranes. (a) Water dispersion of GQD@GO composite. (b) Photographs of GO, GQD@GO, RGO, PQD@RGO membranes. The surface SEM images of (c) GO and (d) GQD@GO membranes. (e) N elemental maps of GQD@GO membranes. Cross-sectional SEM images of (f) GO and (g) GQD@GO membranes. (h) XRD patterns of GO, GQD@GO, RGO, PQD@RGO membranes.
Dynamic contact angle of (a) GO and GQD@GO membranes, (b) RGO and PQD@RGO membranes. Solvent uptake of (c) GO and GQD@GO membranes, (d) RGO and PQD@GO membranes.

Solvent permeance against the viscosity for (a) GO and GQD@GO membranes, (b) RGO and PQD@RGO membranes. (c) Permeance of GO, PQD@GO, and GQD@GO membranes, (d) RGO, PQD@RGO, and GQD@RGO membranes with different solvent.

(a and b) Rejection of several dyes in methanol versus their molecular size, (gray stripes represent primary GO and RGO membranes, orange and blue stripes represent GQD@GO and PQD@RGO membranes, respectively). (c) Acetone permeance and MB rejection of GO and GQD@GO membranes against operation time. (d) Solvent permeance of GQD@GO membrane with and without HCl (1 M) and NaOH (1 M) treatment.

head.ce:title: Manipulating microenvironments of nanochannels in lamellar membranes by quantum dots for highly enhanced nanofiltration performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Institute of Advanced Technology, Zhengzhou University, 97 Wenhua Road, Zhengzhou 450003, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Institute of Advanced Technology, Zhengzhou University, 97 Wenhua Road, Zhengzhou 450003, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemical Engineering, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lamellar membranes are promising for highly selective permeation, among which graphene-based ones are widely studied. However, graphene-based membranes give limited molecule permeance due to the unfavorable microenvironments of nanochannels, mainly single chemical affinity and narrow channel size. Herein, quantum dots (QDs) were introduced into nanochannels to manipulate the microenvironments for optimized performance. Particularly, hydrophobic graphene quantum dots were anchored in hydrophilic graphene oxide nanochannels to improve the affinity towards nonpolar molecules and locally enlarge the channel size from 0.84 to 1.35 nm. The pleasant affinity facilitates rapid dissolution and entry of molecules into nanochannels, and the enlarged channel size promotes their fast transport. This synergistic effect permits remarkably enhanced permeance for both polar and nonpolar molecules, especially for nonpolar ones. Meanwhile, the narrow distance at QD-free area effectively rejects dyes larger than 2.0 nm. Additionally, the strong covalent bonds between QDs and adjacent nanosheets bring superior stability for membranes.

ce:section.ce:para: The demands for high performance separation membranes have overwhelmingly increased due to the global challenges of energy crisis, clean water shortage, and greenhouse effect. Two-dimensional (2D) lamellar membrane, an emerging kind of nanofiltration membrane material featuring well-defined nanochannels, is attractive for various separation fields, such as water and organics purification, seawater desalination, and gas separation (Lively and Sholl, 2017; Shen et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2019). Molecules transport through the lamellar membrane tortuously from in-plane slit-like pores to plane-to-plane interlayer nanochannels, in which the microenvironments, that is, chemical affinity and channel size, play a vital role in determining membrane permeance and selectivity (Zhang et al., 2019; Thebo et al., 2018). To acquire fast permeation and accurate sieving, the channel size should be precisely tuned to separate the targeted substance based on size sieving principle and offer enough spacing for unimpeded molecule transporting (Wang et al., 2018; Hung et al., 2014). Meanwhile, the chemical affinity of channel walls to molecules should be regulated to acquire advantageous dissolution ability and construct facilitated transfer highways (Huang et al., 2015; Ding et al., 2018). Manipulating the transfer nanochannels to acquire high liquid or ion transport is widely used in thin film nanocomposite membranes, especially by incorporating nanofillers that are porous to provide more transfer nanochannels (Zhao et al., 2020a; Zhao et al., 2020b; Zhu et al., 2019). Furthermore, lamellar membranes are expected to acquire outstanding structural stability to meet the practical requirements (Yeh et al., 2015).

Graphene-based 2D materials, especially graphene oxide (GO), have aroused huge interest in various fields. Due to unique one-atomic thickness, various hydrophilic groups, and good chemical stability, GO nanosheets have become the mainstream for lamellar membranes (Xu et al., 2017; Abraham et al., 2017; Lin and Grossman, 2015; Saraswat et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2019). GO membranes are gradually used in organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN) in recent years. As examples, Shi et al. discovered that GO membrane was stable in various organic solvents, and the semipermeability was tunable by modulating the size and surface properties of nanochannels (Huang et al., 2015). Besides, Nunes and coworkers designed GO membrane with alternating dual-spacing channels by in suit intercalating scattered sub-5 nm silica nanoparticles in the GO interlayers. This membrane exhibited simultaneously high methanol permeance and dye rejection (Wang et al., 2019). Moreover, Chung and colleagues used GO membrane to recycle the active pharmaceutical ingredients from isopropanol and acquired good separation capability (Li et al., 2018). Whereas, further improving the solvent permeance of GO membrane is difficult due to some intrinsic defects. Concretely, the interlayer size of pristine GO is about 0.85 nm, which is too narrow to achieve high solvent permeance (e.g. below 15 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 for acetone), and hard to satisfy practical requirements (Yang et al., 2017). Besides, GO membranes are only suitable for transporting polar solvents due to the hydrophilic groups. In contrast, it allows almost no permeance for nonpolar solvents owing to the poor dissolution ability, which impedes the application of GO membranes in most practical mixed solvent systems (Wang et al., 2019). Importantly, GO membranes usually suffer from instability in aqueous and organic media due to the weakness of interactions between adjacent nanosheets (Yang et al., 2017). This leads to the swelling or even disintegration of membrane in operation environment (Yeh et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2018). To solve these drawbacks, numerous efforts have been made, from enlarging the channel size by intercalating large molecules to decreasing transport resistance by reducing membrane thickness (Huang et al., 2013). For instance, Ren et al. inserted tannic acid into GO layers to increase the channel size from 0.76 to 0.99 nm, affording over 10 times increase in water permeance. Moreover, membrane stability was improved because of the crosslinking effect of neighboring GO nanosheets (Thebo et al., 2018). Nair and coworkers acquired an increased water permeance from 2.2 to 8.5 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 by reducing membrane thickness from 35 to 15 nm (Yang et al., 2017). However, rare attention has been payed to the regulation of chemical affinity of membranes. Besides, these inserted molecules are generally hydrophilic ones, similar to the wettability of GO membrane, thus still impeding the permeation of nonpolar molecules. However, the organic system in practical applications is rather sophisticated, containing both polar and nonpolar solvents (Liu et al., 2018; Villalobos et al., 2017). Therefore, constructing highly stable nanochannels with both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions, as well as suitable channel size is in urgent need for optimized lamellar membrane performance, especially for OSN application.

Recently, 0D quantum dots (QDs) with molecular-level size (2–5 nm) and tunable chemical components attract increasingly attention for membrane separation (Wu et al., 2018; Zhen and Wu, 2018; Li et al., 2019). The elaborately tailed small-sized QDs show great potential to be uniformly inserted into the nanochannels of graphene-based lamellar membranes to manipulate the inner microenvironments. Interestingly, highly carbonized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with hydrophobic surface hold promise to build hydrophobic regions within the hydrophilic GO nanochannels. Similarly, carbonization-free polymer-like quantum dots (PQDs) with hydrophilic –CO2H and –NH–/–NH2 groups are expected for constructing hydrophilic zones within hydrophobic reduced graphene oxide (RGO) nanochannels. Hence, these modified nanochannels would show excellent affinity towards both polar and nonpolar solvents, promoting their fast dissolution and entry into membranes. Besides, the enlarged channel size should facilitate the expedite transport of these solvents, endowing lamellar membranes with superior OSN performance. Additionally, the QDs could also act as tiny bridges to covalently bond adjacent nanosheets thus bring strong structural stability. Despite these potential advantages, lamellar membranes with QDs intercalation have been rarely reported until now.

Herein, we report the preparation of QD intercalated-graphene-based lamellar membranes with manipulated nanochannel microenvironments for enhanced nanofiltration performance. Hydrophilic PQDs and hydrophobic GQDs are synthesized by a controllable method. A moderate amount of GQDs are inserted into GO nanochannels to locally enlarge the channel size from 0.84 to 1.35 nm and bring hydrophobic sites. These hydrophobic sites and hydrophilic regions permit the fast dissolution of both nonpolar and polar solvents into the membrane, while the enlarged channel size promotes their rapid diffusion. Particularly, the permeances for acetone and n-hexane reach as high as 173.5 and 85.4 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, respectively, which are more than 5 times higher than primary GO membranes and excess most of the developed lamellar membranes. Moreover, this novel membrane displays superior structural stability even under strong acid or alkali environments. To verify the universality of this strategy, a similar lamellar membrane is prepared by intercalating hydrophilic PQDs into hydrophobic RGO nanochannels, and similar findings are observed. This highly enhanced molecule permeance by accurately manipulating microenvironments of nanochannels paves the way for designing more efficient lamellar membranes for separation applications.

Graphite powders (45 μm), citric acid (CA, ≥99.5%), and diethylenetriamine (DETA, 99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 98 wt%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%), phosphate (H2PO4), nitric acid (HNO3), and hydrochloric (HCl) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemcial Reagent Co. Ltd., China. Potassium permanganate (KMnO4), glycerol, and dimethyl formamide (DMF) were acquired from Kewei Chemistry Co., Ltd. Nylon microfiltration support membrane (pore diameter, 0.2 μm) was supplied by Tianjin Jinteng Experimental equipment Co., Ltd. Dopamine, Tris, and organic solvents (acetone, acetonitrile, methanol, ethanol, isopropanol, n-butanol, n-hexane, n-heptane, and toluene) were obtained from Tianjin Kemiou Chemistry Reagent Co., Ltd. Acid yellow 79 (AY79, 1111 g mol−1), reactive black (RB, 992 g mol−1), evans blue (EB, 960 g mol−1), methylene blue (MB, 374 g mol−1), and methyl orange (MO, 327 g mol−1) were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GO, RGO, GQDs, and PQDs
ce:section.ce:para: GO nanosheets were synthesized from natural graphite powders based on modified Hummers’ method in literature (William et al., 1958). RGO was reduced from GO through Wallace’s method:GO powder was fully dispersed in water to form brown dispersion with concentration of 0.5 mg mL−1. Then, 400 mL GO solution, 400 mL water, 0.16 mL hydrazine solution (80 wt% in water), and 2.88 mL ammonia solution (25 wt%) were mixed in a 1000 mL glass bottle. After being vigorously stirred for 5 min, the bottle was put in a water bath (~95 °C) for 1 h. Then, the RGO dispersion was obtained after cooling to room temperature (Li et al., 2008).

QDs were synthesized via one-pot, microwave-assisted condensation of CA and DETA (Fig. S1). Typically, PQDs were prepared as follows: CA (0.9606 g) and DETA (540 μL) were dissolved in ultra-purified water (2.0 g) under ultrasonic treatment for 5 min at room temperature. Afterward, the mixture was heated in a 750 W microwave oven for 7 min for reaction. Subsequently, the products were washed with ethanol by several times to remove residual small molecules, followed by dispersing in water and freeze-drying of the aqueous solution for 48 h. For preparing GQDs with high carbonization, the reaction solvent of ultra-purified water was replaced by a mixed solvent of water (0.5 g) and glycerol (1.5 g) to provide a higher reaction temperature (above 100 °C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQD@GO and PQD@RGO lamellar composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Nylon support was pre-treated by dopamine aqueous solution (2 mg mL−1, pH = 8.5) for 2 h to deposit an adhesive polydopamine layer to anchor GO nanosheets, which was donated as D-Nylon, and rinsed in water for later utilization (Wang et al., 2018). As illustrated in Fig. 1 , to prepare the lamellar composite membrane, GO solution (50 mL, 1 mg mL−1) and different amounts of GQD solution (50 mL, 100 mL, 150 mL, 200 mL, 1 mg mL−1) were mixed and diluted to 500 mL. This mixed solution was then stirred for another 1 h to completely mix GO and GQDs, and then further stirred overnight at 70 °C for reaction. Next, the dispersion was filtrated through the D-nylon support in vacuum filtration cell and the resulted lamellar composite membrane was denoted as GQD@GO membrane. Similarly, the PQD@RGO membrane was fabricated by inserting PQDs into RGO nanochannels. For comparison, GO and RGO membranes were prepared through the same procedure but without the addition of GQD/PQD.

The morphologies of GO, RGO, QDs, and membranes were characterized via transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G220 S-TWIN), scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Nanosem 430), and atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker Dimension FastScan). The chemical structure and element content were probed by fourier transform infrared (FTIR, PerkinElmer Spectrum Two instrument), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS Supra, Kratos, UK), and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) on SEM. X-ray diffraction (XRD) of membranes was probed using a Bruker D8 Advance ECO in the range of 5−60°. Water and diiodomethane contact angles were measured with FACE (model OCA 25, Germany).

Firstly, the membrane sample was dried in an oven at 60 °C for 48 h and then tailored into a square shaped sample (about 1.5 cm × 1.5 cm). Subsequently, the weight (Wdry , g) was measured. Then, the sample was immersed in certain solvent for 72 h at room temperature until a complete equilibrium. Afterwards, the swollen membrane sample was taken out and wiped away the excess solvent with a tissue paper, and the weight (Wwet , g) was re-measured. The solvent uptake was calculated as below:

Solvent uptake (%) = (Wwet
– Wdry )/Wdry × 100

Nanofiltration performance (permeance and rejection) of membranes was tested using a homemade dead-end apparatus with a volume of 500 mL (Fig. S2). The apparatus was equipped with magnetic stirring (300 rpm) and the testing was conducted in a N2 pressurized cell under 1.0 bar. For permeance measurement, pure organic solvent was used as feed solution, and the filtered solvent was collected after reaching a steady state. As for dye rejection, the dye was dissolved in ethanol with a concentration of 100 ppm to serve as feed solution. Then 450 mL of the feed solution was slowly added into the filtration apparatus and the filtered solution was also collected after reaching a steady state to avoid the adsorption of dyes. To ensure the stability of membrane, the final results of permeance and rejection were obtained by testing three identical membranes. The permeance (L m−2 h−1 bar−1) was calculated from V / (A × t × P), where V, A, t, and P denoted the permeate volume (L), effective membrane area (m2), testing time (h), and operation pressure (bar), respectively. The rejection (%) was obtained from (1 − Cp /Cf ) × 100, where Cp and Cf referred to the concentration of filtered side solution and pristine feed solution, respectively, which were determined by UV–vis technique.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of GQD@GO and PQD@RGO composites
ce:section.ce:para: GO nanosheets were synthesized from graphene powder through modified Hummers’ method. TEM (Fig. 2 a) and AFM (Fig. S3) images reveal that GO nanosheets possess typical flexible sheet structure with irregular wrinkles. The lateral size and thickness of GO are about 3 µm and 1.4 nm, respectively. Since the theoretical thickness of a single-layer GO is 0.8 nm (Zhu et al., 2018), one or two water layers are absorbed on nanosheet surface, which is benefited from the hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups. This can be verified by the high O/C ratio of 53% as calculated by XPS (Fig. S4) and the low water contact angle of 41.5° for pressed tablet of GO (Fig. S5). Then, the GO was reduced to produce RGO through Wallace’s method, during which the intact sheet structure was well maintained (Fig. S6). The O/C ration of RGO is decreased to 13%, verifying that a large amount of oxygen-containing functional groups are reduced (Fig. S4). This reduction process results in higher water contact angle of 91.3° for pressed tablet of RGO (Fig. S5), implying its hydrophobic character.

QDs were synthesized by controllable condensation of CA and DETA (Fig. S1), during which the chemical components were facilely regulated by adjusting reaction solvent (i.e. reaction temperature). Hydrophilic PQDs were fabricated by using water as solvent to ensure carbonization-free condensation, thus bring numerous evenly-dispersed –CO2H and –NH–/–NH2 groups (Fig. S1). Hydrophobic GQDs were fabricated by further carbonizing these O- or N-containing groups by utilizing glycerol/water mixture (mass ratio: 3:1) as solvent to generate high reaction temperature (above 100 °C). Here, the carbonization degree of GQDs is controlled to ensure a few groups on their surface, which are ready for the reaction with GO. TEM image (Fig. 2b) reveals that GQDs are typical 0 D materials with small size of 2–5 nm, and they possess clear lattice fringes due to the high carbonization. While the carbonization-free PQDs could not be detected by TEM due to their disordered stacking and aggregation. This nanostructure can be further confirmed by the crystalline band at around 21° on XRD curves (Fig. S7). Moreover, XPS (Fig. 2e) and FTIR (Fig. 2f) results obviously declare the various hydrophilic groups for both PQDs and GQDs, in which the intensity of –C = O and C–N bonds in GQDs are much weaker than that in PQDs. This distinction further brings difference in wettability, as proved by water contact angles (Fig. S8). The low value of 29.5° for PQDs suggests the highly hydrophilic character, while the GQDs also exhibit an elevated but relatively small water contact angle of 42.1° due to their limited hydrophilic groups.

Next, GQD@GO composite was prepared by the reaction between GO and GQD (Fig. 1). Fig. 2c shows that QDs are firmly and uniformly anchored on the nanosheet surface, even suffering from ultrasonic treatment for more than 20 min during the sample preparation for TEM testing. This should be benefited from the strong covalent interactions between them (Fig. S9), and the chemical cross-linking reaction is identified by XPS and FTIR results (Thebo et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2018). Here, to expediently identify the covalent bonding between QDs and nanosheets, a composite of PQD@GO was synthesized accordingly and used for the characterization of chemical composition by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2d, GO nanosheets show the typical XPS spectra as those reported in literatures (Thebo et al., 2018; Abraham et al., 2017), which contain four components that correspond to the carbon atoms in hydroxyl, epoxy, carbonyl, and carboxyl groups. After reaction with PQDs, the O/C atomic ratio reduces from 53% for primary GO to 44% for PQD@GO composite, even though PQDs are rich with oxygen-containing groups, which possess a high O/C atomic ratio of 56%. This observation gives a strong evidence that the functional groups on GO have been reduced by PQDs, forming covalent bonds. Analogously, GQDs and PQDs are able to covalently bond on GO and RGO surface respectively under the same condition. This cross-linking reaction can be further proved by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2f). Compared with GO and RGO nanosheets, GQD@GO and PQD@RGO composites show a strong new peak at 1533 cm−1, which corresponds to the stretching vibration of the amide groups (–C–N–). This peak is also observed on the spectra of PQDs and GQDs but shows much weaker intensity, and the feeble peak on GO spectrum should be due to the impurities introduced during GO preparation procedure. This increased peak intensity confirms that the carboxyl groups on nanosheets are covalently bonded with the amidogen groups of QDs, forming more amide groups. Besides, the peak intensities of –C = O and C–O–C groups on the spectra of nanosheets are obviously weakened as compared with their composites, which indicates that these groups are consumed during reaction with QDs. These evidences collectively confirm that there exist strong covalent interactions between nanosheets and QDs in the GQD@GO and PQD@RGO composites. Apart from the covalent interaction, there should also exist hydrogen-bonding interactions and π–π interactions between nanosheets and QDs, collectively contributing to stable GQD@GO and PQD@RGO composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologies and physicochemical properties of lamellar composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Next, GQD@GO composite was uniformly dispersed into water and filtered on D-Nylon support to prepare 2D lamellar composite membranes (Fig. 3 a). The photographs show that these membranes are intact with no obvious defects (Fig. 3b), and the intercalation of GQDs turns the color of pristine GO membranes from bright golden to dark golden. The precise morphologies are detected by SEM (Fig. 3c), which reveals that the surface of pristine GO membrane is rather smooth with many wave-like wrinkles. This is close to the observation in literatures (Wang et al., 2019; Cheng et al., 2019). While Fig. 3d shows that the surface becomes much smoother with less wrinkles after intercalating GQDs. This finding should be due to the crosslinking effect of GQDs that firmly fixes adjacent GO layers and thus drags these wrinkles into plain area. Moreover, the presence of GQDs is further identified by EDS (Fig. 3e), which reveals large amount of N elements from GQDs on the surface of GQD@GO membrane. Cross-sectional SEM image (Fig. 3f) clearly shows the typical layer-by-layer structure of GO membrane as reported in literatures (Wang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2014) and the thickness is ~ 500 nm. Inserting GQDs into GO layers maintains its laminar structure and substantially smooth the wave-like wrinkles (Fig. 3g), which matches the results of surface SEM image. Moreover, the membrane thickness is controlled by filtration volume, that is, nanosheet loading (Fig. S10). And the thickness of ~500 nm was selected for membrane characterization and performance investigation considering its favorable nanofiltration performance and outstanding structural stability.

The nanostructure of membranes is further characterized by XRD, as depicted in Fig. 3h. All membranes show a sharp diffraction peak, confirming the ordered stacking of nanosheets, which matches well with the results of SEM. Particularly, GO membrane possesses a peak at 2θ of 10.5°, corresponding to the channel size of 0.84 nm (Yang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2016). This peak pronouncedly shifts to 6.6°, suggesting the enlarged size of 1.35 nm for GQD@GO membrane. Analogously, RGO membrane acquires a size increase from 0.38 to 0.95 nm for the PQD@RGO membrane. The larger interlayer distance implies that the GQDs/PQDs are intercalated between adjacent GO/RGO nanochannels as spacer in most regions of the lamellar composite membranes.

The intercalated QDs markedly adjust the chemical affinity of lamellar composite membranes to polar and nonpolar solvents. This can be demonstrated by the results of time-dependent solvent contact angles, as shown in Fig. 4 a. Due to the highly hydrophilic feature of GO membrane, it gives high and stable diiodomethane contact angle of over 95°. While the water contact angle is 62.7° at the first contact and then quickly reduces to 32.1° within 30 s for GQD@GO membrane, implying an enhanced dissolution ability for nonpolar solvents. Besides, GQD@GO composite membrane displays low water contact angle of 29.6°, demonstrating its pleasant affinity towards both polar and nonpolar solvents. This can be further identified by the solvent uptake of membranes. Here, a reference PQD@GO membrane was prepared by introducing hydrophilic PQDs into GO nanochannels with the channel size of about 1.3 nm (Fig. S11), which is close to that of GQD@GO membrane. This PQD@GO membrane is designed to emphatically identify the effect of chemical affinity to membrane performance. Fig. 4c shows that comparing with primary GO membrane, inserting QDs pronouncedly increases solvent uptake (nearly 60% for methanol) due to the enlarged channel size. Significantly, hydrophobic GQDs distinctly intensify the nonpolar solvent uptake of toluene from 8.8% for GQD@GO membrane to 17.8% for PQD@GO membrane. This directly confirms the vital effect of well-defined chemical affinity on fast molecular dissolution into membrane. Following the similar architecture, hydrophilic PQDs significantly intensify the affinity of hydrophobic RGO laminate towards polar solvents. This can be verified by the fast-declined water contact angle from 53.7° to 28.9° within 30 s, and the increased acetone uptake from 2.9% to 17.1% (Fig. 4b and Fig. 4d). Therefore, PQD@RGO membrane also acquires outstanding affinity towards both polar and nonpolar solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanofiltration performance of lamellar composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The organic solvent nanofiltration performance of membranes was evaluated by solvent permeation, dyes rejection, and operational stability. It should be noted that the D-nylon support with large pores (around 200 nm) gives high molecular permeance (above 2000 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 for water) but negligible dye rejection. Fig. 5 a displays that hydrophilic GO membrane gives relatively high permeance towards polar solvents but almost no permeance to nonpolar ones. For instance, acetone and acetonitrile acquire the highest permeances of 32.1 and 30.3 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, respectively, while n-hexane and toluene only obtain the permeances of 2.8 and 2.3 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, respectively. In contrast, inserting QDs notably boosts the permeance for both polar and nonpolar solvents. Particularly, the permeance for n-hexane is increased by more than 30 times, reaching 85.4 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, and toluene permeance is also obviously increased from 2.3 to 63.6 L m−2h−1 bar−1. Besides, the permeances for acetone and methanol achieve as high as 173.5 and 146.7 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, respectively. Additionally, Fig. 5c shows that the GQD@GO membrane achieves much higher permeance for nonpolar solvents (85.4 m−2 h−1 bar−1 for n-hexane) than that of hydrophilic PQD@GO membrane (30.6 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 for n-hexane). These results directly demonstrate the crucial effect of suitable chemical affinity on superior solvent permeance, which determines the readily dissolution and entry of molecules into membranes.

The outstanding permeance of GQD@GO membrane for both polar and nonpolar solvents should originate from the optimized physicochemical microenvironments of nanochannels. As demonstrated above, the GQD-decorated GO nanochannels possess both hydrophilic and hydrophobic regions, which show excellent affinity towards both polar and nonpolar solvents. This would facilitate the dissolution of solvents into membrane, and then the enlarged channel size promotes their fast transport along nanochannels. Thus, a highly-effective synergistic transport process is established, contributing to outstanding permeance for both polar and nonpolar solvents. Similarly, intercalating hydrophilic PQDs into hydrophobic RGO nanochannels evaluates the affinity of RGO membrane to polar solvents and substantially improves their permeance (Fig. 5b and Fig. 5d). Together with the enlarged nanochannels, PQD@RGO membrane also acquires high permeance for polar and nonpolar solvents. And the permeances for acetone and n-hexane reach 106.3 and 133.1 L m−2 h−1 bar−1, respectively. It is worth noting that the permeance of prepared graphene-based membranes excesses most reported lamellar membranes under identical rejection (Fig. S12). This highlights the superiority of regulating physicochemical microenvironments of nanochannels in enhancing the nanofiltration performance for lamellar membranes.

The rejection ability of composite membranes was determined by separating five dyes with different sizes (Table S1), as depicted in Fig. 6 a and Fig. 6b. It is worth noting that the membranes were immersed into dye solutions for around 3 h, and the UV–vis absorption spectra display almost no variation before and after immersion (Fig. S13), indicating the negligible adsorption and electrostatic attraction of dyes with membranes (Wu et al., 2019; Ding et al., 2017). While few dyes form weak electrostatic repulsion with membranes, which would facilitate their rejection to some degree. Fig. 6a reveals that comparing with primary GO membrane, the enlarged channel size of GQD@GO membrane brings relatively lower rejection for dyes with size smaller than 1.4 nm (42.4% for MB). When altering the size to 2.0 nm, the nanochannels can block most of RB with rejection of over 95%. For the dyes with larger size, membrane rejection reaches nearly 100%. Moreover, similar rejection behaviors are observed for PQD@RGO membrane. Compared with primary GO/RGO membranes, the corresponding composite membranes acquire close rejection ability to dyes with size larger than 2.0 nm. This precise rejection of these lamellar composite membranes should be originated from the small channel size that prevents the entry of large dyes into membrane. Besides, the inserted QDs act as molecular sieves to block the dyes transport. Moreover, the narrow QD-free areas as well as the pristine domains with strong π–π attraction can also restrict large dyes. It is worth noting that the rejection ability of as-prepared membranes meets the requirements of nanofiltration process, holding great promise for OSN applications (Prozorovska and Kidambi, 2018; Huang et al., 2020).

Additionally, the structural stability of membranes is inspected by immersing membranes into various solvents for more than one week and the inner structure is tested by XRD. Fig. S14 clearly shows that the typical crystalline peak (2θ = 10.50) of primary GO membrane shifts to lower values after immersing in polar water and acetone. This observation suggests that the layered structure is collapsed due to the entry of polar solvents, which induces the swelling of layered nanosheets, while the nonpolar solvents produce almost no variation to layered structure of membranes. This is due to that hydrophilic property of GO membrane shows strong affinity to polar molecules but repulsion to nonpolar ones. Such phenomenon is commonly reported for GO membranes, which is one of the crucial factors that restricts membrane applications (Zhang et al., 2020; Zheng et al., 2020). In contrast, the strong covalent bonds between GQDs and adjacent GO layers restrict the movements of GO nanosheets, giving superior structural stability. This further offers excellent operation stability for GQD@GO composite membrane, as verified by the continuous 60 h operation process (Fig. 6c). The acetone permeance and MB rejection of GQD@GO membrane keep almost unchanged during the long-term operation process. While severe swelling of GO membrane gives sharp decrease in MB rejection and increase in acetone permeance. In addition, accordant behaviors are detected by RGO and PQD@RGO membranes as illustrated in Fig. S15. Furthermore, to detect the application potential of as-prepared lamellar composite membranes in practical processes, the membranes are soaked in 1 M HCl and NaOH solutions for 30 min. Fig. 6d reveals that the membranes also exhibit stable structure with negligible permeance alteration (<4%), while traditional lamellar membranes fail to do so.

In summary, QDs were introduced into the nanochannels of graphene-based lamellar membranes to regulate the microenvironments and optimize the nanofiltration performance. GQDs were introduced into GO nanochannels to anchor hydrophobic sites within hydrophilic nanochannels, aiming to enhance membrane affinity to nonpolar solvents. The well-designed amphipathic nanodomains readily facilitate the dissolution of both polar and nonpolar solvents into membrane, while the enlarged channel size (from 0.84 to 1.35 nm) promotes their rapid migration driven by pressure. This highly synergized transport process contributes to outstanding solvent permeances: 173.5 and 85.4 L m−2 h−1 bar−1 for acetone and n-hexane, respectively. Accordingly, the chemical affinity is demonstrated to be the primary factor that determines molecular permeance since it governs molecule entry into nanochannels. To verify this observation, hydrophilic PQDs were inserted into hydrophobic RGO nanochannels and similar effect was achieved. Moreover, the as-prepared graphene-based lamellar membranes exhibit excellent rejection ability for dyes with size larger than 2.0 nm. And these membranes show superior structural stability even under strong acidic or alkaline environments. This strategy of manipulating microenvironments of nanochannels provides a new avenue for designing advanced lamellar membranes in separation applications.

Xiaoli Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Shiyuan Liu: Visualization, Software, Formal analysis. Xulin Cui: Investigation, Software. Jianlong Lin: Software. Haoqin Zhang: Validation, Resources, Funding acquisition. Jie Zhang: Project administration, Supervision. Jingtao Wang: Conceptualization, Validation, Writing - review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U1804127), Natural Science Foundation of Henan Province (182300410276), Training Plan for Young Backbone Teachers in Universities of Henan Province (2018GGJS003). Center for advanced analysis and computational science, Zhengzhou University is also highly acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ces.2020.116001.
